text.title: regulatory-compliance-2026 Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for regulatory-compliance-2026. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: US government entities must comply with ADA Title II at WCAG 2.1 AA by April 24, 2026 (large entities, 50K+ population) or April 24, 2027 (smaller entities).
rule.rationale: DOJ final rule published March 2024. Non-compliance exposes state/local agencies to DOJ investigation, private lawsuits, and loss of federal funding.

rule.statement: EAA requires WCAG 2.1 AA (via EN 301 549) for new e-commerce, banking, transport, and e-book products from June 28, 2025; existing products have until June 28, 2030.
rule.rationale: EU member states set their own enforcement; fines up to 4% of annual revenue. Web Accessibility Directive already applies to all public-sector websites.

rule.statement: Every public-facing product must publish an accessibility statement that includes conformance level, known limitations, contact information, and the date of the last review.
rule.rationale: Required by ADA Title II (DOJ rule), EU Web Accessibility Directive, and EAA. The statement is the primary compliance evidence regulators and plaintiffs request first.

rule.statement: Produce a Voluntary Product Accessibility Template (VPAT) — also called an Accessibility Conformance Report (ACR) — for every product used in government or enterprise procurement; update annually or after major changes.
rule.rationale: US Section 508 and ADA Title II procurement standards require VPAT evidence. EU public sector procurement increasingly requires EN 301 549 ACRs. Without one, vendors are excluded from bids.

rule.statement: Organizations must maintain an internal accessibility policy documenting organizational commitment, responsible parties, testing cadence, training requirements, and procurement standards.
rule.rationale: DOJ consent decrees and EU member state enforcement demand evidence of systemic process, not one-off fixes. Policy is the paper trail that proves ongoing effort.

rule.statement: Exemptions are narrow, must be individually documented, and always require an accessible alternative to be provided.
rule.rationale: Regulators and courts reject blanket exemptions. Each exemption needs a written justification, scope limitation, and alternative access path.
